perturbation.original_text: As used herein, “Proprietary Information” means information that (i) embodies trade secrets as defined under 18 U.S.C. § 1839 or (ii) is commercial or financial information that is privileged or confidential under the Freedom of Information Act (5 U.S.C. § 552(b)(4)), and that is developed at private expense outside this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: As used herein, “Proprietary Information” means information that (i) embodies trade secrets as defined under 18 U.S.C. § 1839 or (ii) is commercial or financial information, regardless of source or context, and that is developed at private expense outside this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The removal of the phrase "that is privileged or confidential under the Freedom of Information Act (5 U.S.C. § 552(b)(4))" broadens the definition of Proprietary Information in a way that conflicts with the FOIA's intent to make government information accessible.
perturbation.law_explanation: By omitting the reference to FOIA exemptions, the contract's definition of "Proprietary Information" could be interpreted to include information that the FOIA requires to be disclosed, directly contradicting the law's intent regarding public access to government information.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: ent: Privacy PIE with EFF's Andrew Crocker From the Organizers:We're excited to welcome Andrew Crocker, Surveillance Litigation Director for the EFF, as this month's EFA Edition of Privacy PIE! As Surveillance Litigation... EFF has filed an amicus brief in Trabajadores v. Bessent , a case concerning the Internal Revenue Service (IRS) sharing protected personal tax information with the Department of Homeland Security for the purposes of immigration enforcement. Our expertise in privacy and data sharing makes us the ideal organization to step in... For the first time, EFF is excited to be at The Diana Initiative ! Be sure to stop by our info table at the conference to learn more about our latest work defending digital freedom for all. We're going to be handing out stickers and spreading the word about privacy... EFF is excited to support SeaGL (the Seattle GNU/Linux Conference )! Taking place at the University of Washington, SeaGL is your one-stop-shop to learn about free and open source software and hardware. SeaGL is free to attend , with no registration required. Just show up and enjoy the community! More about ... The State of Washington enacted one of the strongest consumer data privacy laws in recent years: the “My Health, My Data” Act. While the law only applies to one category of personal data—our health information—its structure could be used to protect all manner of data. Keeping up on the latest digital rights news has never been easier. With a new look, EFF's EFFector newsletter covers the latest details on our work defending your rights to privacy and free expression online. EFFector 37.7 covers some of the very sneaky tactics that Meta has been using... The cryptography that protects our privacy and security online relies on the fact that even the strongest computers will take essentially forever to do certain tasks, like factoring prime numbers and finding discrete logarithms which are important for RSA encryption , Diffie-Hellman key ... perturbation.justification: The changed text broadens the definition of "Proprietary Information" by removing the clause "that is privileged or confidential under the Freedom of Information Act (5 U.S.C. § 552(b)(4))". This omission directly contradicts the intent of FOIA, which aims to make government information accessible to the public, as referenced in Scraped Law Snippet 1: "5 USC 552 : Public information; agency rules, opinions, orders, records, and proceedings (a) Each agency shall make available to the public information as follows:". By removing the reference to FOIA exemptions, the contract now potentially includes information that should be publicly available, thus creating a legal contradiction as it undermines the public's right to access government information.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party will treat Proprietary Information and NREL Protected Information that is withi n t he Scope and that is disclosed in compliance with Paragraph 2.a, above, as confidential an d pr oprietary and will use such information only for the Purpose. Receiving Party will not disclos e s uch information to any third party for the duration of the Confidentiality Period without the prior written approval of Disclosing Party. Notwithstanding the foregoing, Proprietary Informati on pr ovided to Alliance hereunder is subject to inspection by DOE or its designee upon reasonabl e not ice. Proprietary Information provided to DOE employees is protected against further disclosur e under 18 U .S.C. § 1905.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party will treat Proprietary Information and NREL Protected Information that is withi n t he Scope and that is disclosed in compliance with Paragraph 2.a, above, as confidential an d pr oprietary and will use such information only for the Purpose. Receiving Party may disclos e s uch information to any third party, and Proprietary Information provided to DOE employees is not protected against further disclosur e under 18 U .S.C. § 1905.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement for prior written approval for third-party disclosure and the protection under 18 U.S.C. § 1905, the modified text conflicts directly with the legal protections afforded to confidential government information and trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification directly contradicts 18 U.S.C. § 1905, which prohibits the unauthorized disclosure of confidential information by government employees. The removal of the protection against further disclosure violates this statute.

Disclosure of confidential information generally Whoever, being an officer or employee of the United States or of any department or agency thereof, any person acting on behalf of the Federal Housing Finance Agency, or agent of the Department of Justice as defined in the Antitrust Civil Process Act ( 15 U.S.C. 1311–1314 ), or being an employee of a private sector org
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts 18 U.S.C. § 1905, as it explicitly removes the protection against further disclosure of Proprietary Information provided to DOE employees. The original text stated that such information 'is protected against further disclosure under 18 U.S.C. § 1905,' while the changed text states that it is 'not protected against further disclosure under 18 U.S.C. § 1905.' The scraped law snippet confirms that 18 U.S.C. § 1905 pertains to the 'Disclosure of confidential information generally,' which makes the removal of this protection a clear violation. The modification removes a core legal obligation, resulting in a direct and serious legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: A Party receiving Proprietary Information and/or NREL Protected Information will adhere to U.S. Export Administration Laws and Regulations and will not export or re- export any such Proprietary Information and/or NREL Protected Information, any technical data, items, or products arising from such information to any country or person unless properly authorized by the U.S. Government.
perturbation.changed_text: A Party receiving Proprietary Information and/or NREL Protected Information may export or re-export any such Proprietary Information and/or NREL Protected Information, any technical data, items, or products arising from such information to any country or person regardless of U.S. Export Administration Laws and Regulations.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the obligation to comply with U.S. export control laws, directly contradicting those laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: By stating that parties may export information regardless of U.S. export control laws, the modified text directly contradicts the EAR, which strictly regulates the export and re-export of certain technologies and information for national security and foreign policy reasons.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: EAR | Bureau of Industry and Security An official website of the United States government Here's how you know Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Skip to main content Export Administration Regulations (EAR) This tool helps you navigate the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. Commerce Control List (CCL) The Commerce Control List (CCL) (Supplement No. 1 to Part 774 of the EAR) is divided into ten broad categories, and each category is further subdivided into five product groups. Additionally, you may explore our interactive tool . Category 0 - Nuclear Materials Facilities & Equipment [and Miscellaneous Items] Category 1 - Special Materials and Related Equipment, Chemicals, “Microorganisms,” and “Toxins” Category 2 - Materials Processing Category 3 - Electronics Category 4 - Computers Category 5 Part 1 - Telecommunications Category 5 Part 2 - Information Security Category 6 - Sensors and Lasers Category 7 - Navigation and Avionics Category 8 - Marine Category 9 - Aerospace and Propulsion Legal limitations of the tools on the BIS website The tools on the BIS website are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations , nor are they a primer on how to get started. For that introductory guidance, please visit our Learn and Support page. The tools are an editorial compilation of material and not an official legal edition of the Code of Federal Regulations or the Federal Register . We have made every effort to ensure the material presented in these tools are accurate, but if you are relying on them for legal research you should consult the official editions of those sources to confirm your findings. Nothing in these tools binds BIS or creates any rights, benefits, or defenses, substantive or procedural, that are enforceable by any party in any manner. If you find any errors, please email us.

Enhanced Content - Developer Tools eCFR Content The Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) is the official legal print publication containing the codification of the general and permanent rules published in the Federal Register by the departments and agencies of the Federal Government. The Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (eCFR) is a continuously updated online version of the CFR. It is not an official legal edition of the CFR. Learn more about the eCFR, its status, and the editorial process. perturbation.justification: The modified text explicitly allows the export or re-export of proprietary information regardless of U.S. Export Administration Laws and Regulations, directly contradicting the purpose of the EAR. Scraped Law Snippet 1 clearly identifies the EAR as a tool to navigate export regulations, and Snippet 2 identifies the EAR falls under Title 15 (Commerce and Foreign Trade), Chapter VII (Bureau of Industry and Security, Department of Commerce) Subchapter C, which addresses export administration regulations. The changed text, by permitting exports irrespective of these laws, creates a clear contradiction as it negates the legal obligation to comply with export controls.

perturbation.original_text: Nothing in this Agr eement prohibit s or otherwise restrict s employees or subcontractors of the Parties from lawfully reporting waste, fraud, or abuse related to the performance of a government contract to a designated investigative or law enforcement representative of a federal department or agency authorized to receive such information (e.g., agency Office of the Inspector General).
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement prohibits employees or subcontractors of the Parties from reporting waste, fraud, or abuse related to the performance of a government contract to any representative of a federal department or agency.
perturbation.explanation: This change directly prohibits whistleblowing, contradicting federal laws protecting whistleblowers.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification directly violates the Whistleblower Protection Act, which protects federal employees and others from retaliation for reporting waste, fraud, and abuse. Prohibiting such reporting is a direct contravention of the law's purpose.

perturbation.justification: The changed text directly prohibits employees from reporting waste, fraud, or abuse, which is a direct contravention of whistleblower protection laws. Scraped Law Snippet 1 lists numerous statutes designed to protect whistleblowers, indicating a broad legal effort to encourage and safeguard such reporting. The original text explicitly allowed such reporting, while the changed text forbids it, thus undermining the legal protections and creating a clear legal contradiction.
